metadata.course_name: Stanford CS336 Language Modeling from Scratch

discussions.title: Course introduction and staff
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section introduces the course CS 336 "Language Models From Scratch" and the instructors and course staff. Learners will know who teaches the course and the course goal: to show the full pipeline of building language models end-to-end.

{Definitions}:
- Course: A class named CS 336 that teaches how to build language models from scratch, covering data systems and modeling.

{Key Concepts}:
- The course is taught by Percy and Tatsu with support from TAs and CAs.
- The class aims to let students build language models from the ground up so they understand how every part works.

{Important Notes}:
- Lectures will be shared publicly on YouTube so people outside the class can learn.
- The course is hands-on and expects active coding and systems work.

discussions.title: Why build language models from scratch (motivation)
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This part explains why the instructors teach building models from scratch: researchers are becoming detached from low-level details because many people now just prompt closed models. Building from scratch teaches the real workings and supports fundamental research. The instructors believe you learn core ideas best by implementing them yourself.

{Definitions}:
- Prompting: Giving a prepared input string to a pre-built model to get a response, without changing the model weights.
- Abstraction (in this context): A simple interface (like string in, string out) that hides the inner workings of a system.

{Key Concepts}:
- Prompting and APIs let many people use models but hide important engineering and scientific details.
- Some research needs tearing up the whole stack (data, systems, model) and co-designing parts together.
- Building end-to-end helps researchers understand trade-offs that are invisible when only prompting closed models.

{Important Notes}:
- Relying only on prompting can hide important failures and limits of models.
- The course focuses on teaching tools and intuition that support deep research, not just how to use APIs.

discussions.title: Limits of industrial-scale models and why small-scale results can mislead
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This segment describes how modern large models are expensive and often closed, and why small models can give misleading results. Two examples show that (1) compute spent on attention vs MLP layers changes with scale and (2) emergent behaviors appear only after large scaling, so small-model experiments may not generalize.

{Definitions}:
- Parameter: A single numeric value in a model that is learned during training.
- FLOPs: Short for floating-point operations; a measure of compute work done while training or running models.
- Emergent behavior: A capability or pattern that only appears after a model reaches a certain size or training scale.

{Key Concepts}:
- Frontier models (very large models) cost huge amounts to train and often are not fully documented by their creators.
- At small scales, attention and MLP layers can have similar compute cost, but at large scales MLPs may dominate compute, changing which optimizations matter.
- Some behaviors (like in-context learning) show up only after scaling far enough, so small experiments can miss them.

{Important Notes}:
- You can still learn many technical skills at small scale, but be careful about which conclusions you draw about large-scale behavior.
- The course will teach mechanics and mindset, but some large-scale intuitions can only be learned partially.

discussions.title: Three types of knowledge the class aims to teach
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
The instructors divide useful knowledge into three kinds: mechanics, mindset, and intuitions. Mechanics are concrete building blocks (e.g., transformers, parallelism). Mindset is how to think about efficiency and scaling. Intuitions are empirical rules about what data and modeling choices work, which are harder to teach fully.

{Definitions}:
- Mechanics: Concrete technical skills and components (models, optimizers, systems) that you can implement and measure.
- Mindset: The way of thinking engineers and researchers use, such as focusing on efficiency and scaling trade-offs.
- Intuitions: Experience-based judgments about which datasets, architectures, or hyperparameters tend to work well.

{Key Concepts}:
- Mechanics can be taught well: you will implement transformers and system components.
- Mindset emphasizes squeezing value from hardware and taking scaling seriously.
- Intuitions about what works at large scale are only partially teachable because scale can change which choices succeed.

{Important Notes}:
- The course will aim to give mechanics and mindset strongly, and partial exposure to large-scale intuitions.
- Expect to learn practical efficiency-minded approaches, not just theoretical ideas.

discussions.title: Understanding the 'bitter lesson' and importance of algorithmic efficiency
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This segment clarifies the "bitter lesson": scaling matters, but algorithms and efficiency also matter a lot. At large cost and scale, being algorithmically and operationally efficient becomes crucial because wasted compute is very expensive. Historical algorithmic improvements gave huge speedups beyond hardware growth.

{Definitions}:
- Bitter lesson (rephrased): The idea that general methods that use more compute scale better over time, but the correct view is "algorithms at scale matter"—efficient algorithms plus scale give best results.
- Algorithmic efficiency: How well an algorithm uses compute and data to reach high accuracy; better efficiency saves resources.

{Key Concepts}:
- Accuracy depends on both the compute resources you spend and how efficiently your algorithms use that compute.
- Algorithmic improvements (2012–2019) gave large speedups (e.g., 44x) in training time for some tasks, often outpacing hardware-only progress.
- At frontier scale (hundreds of millions to billions spent), small inefficiencies translate to huge monetary cost.

{Important Notes}:
- Don’t interpret scaling as meaning "just throw money at the problem"; improving algorithms and systems is essential.
- The course will emphasize maximizing efficiency given compute and data budgets.

discussions.title: History and current landscape of language models
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section gives a brief history of language models and the recent rise of foundation and closed frontier models. It explains how past research built many ingredients (attention, optimizers, model parallelism) that led to modern scaling and the split between closed and open models.

{Definitions}:
- Language model: A model that assigns probabilities to sequences of text and can predict next tokens.
- Foundation model: A large model trained on broad data that can be adapted to many downstream tasks (examples: ELMo, BERT, T5).
- Closed model: A model whose weights and training details are not publicly shared and may be available only via an API.
- Open weights model: A model where the learned weights are published for others to run and study.

{Key Concepts}:
- Early large-scale language work included n-gram models trained on huge token counts; modern models differ in architectures and capabilities.
- Key ingredients developed over years: neural language models, sequence models (RNNs), attention, Adam optimizer, and efforts on model parallelism.
- The community now has a mix of closed frontier models (large and private) and open-source efforts that publish weights and sometimes data.

{Important Notes}:
- There are different levels of openness: fully closed, open weights (no data), and fully open (weights + data + code).
- Learning to build models yourself helps fill gaps left by incomplete public documentation of large closed models.

discussions.title: Executable lecture demo and course logistics
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
The instructor shows an "executable lecture" that embeds runnable code and explains course logistics such as assignments, workload, and who the class is for. The course is heavy on assignments, expects students to build many pieces from scratch, and provides unit tests and cluster access.

{Definitions}:
- Executable lecture: A lecture presented as a program that can run code, show environment state, and link to definitions while teaching.
- Unit tests: Small automated checks that validate parts of student code for correctness.
- H100: A modern GPU (NVIDIA H100) used for heavy model training; the class has cluster access to such GPUs.

{Key Concepts}:
- The class is demanding: five units of work and substantial programming and systems work; one student compared the workload to multiple other classes combined.
- Assignments are built-from-scratch: students get minimal scaffolding and must implement components but unit tests and adapters help validate work.
- Use the provided cluster for benchmarks, but do local prototyping first to avoid wasting cluster time.

{Important Notes}:
- The class is for students who want to deeply understand ML systems; it is not the best choice if you want to quickly try fine-tuning or do other research concurrently.
- Start assignments early because the cluster will be busy toward deadlines and read the cluster usage guide carefully.
- AI coding tools can be used carefully, but students are responsible for their own learning and must avoid over-relying on tools to do the work.

discussions.title: Course units overview and the Basics unit
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
The instructor presents the five main course pillars: Basics, Systems, Scaling Laws, Data, and Alignment. The Basics unit gets a full pipeline working: tokenizer, model architecture (transformer), optimizer, and training loop, plus a leaderboard to minimize perplexity.

{Definitions}:
- Tokenizer: A tool that converts text strings into sequences of integers (tokens) and back again.
- Transformer: A neural network architecture based on attention that is the backbone of modern language models.
- BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) tokenizer: A tokenizer that learns token pieces from data by repeatedly merging common pairs of bytes or symbols.
- Optimizer (e.g., AdamW): An algorithm that updates model parameters during training to reduce loss.
- Perplexity: A common language model evaluation metric that measures how well a model predicts a sample; lower is better.

{Key Concepts}:
- Basics teaches tokenization (BPE), the original transformer, cross-entropy loss, and an optimizer like AdamW, plus training loops in PyTorch.
- Many small architectural improvements (activation functions, positional embeddings, normalization choices) matter once combined.
- Assignments include implementing BPE tokenizer and a transformer from limited allowed PyTorch primitives, and a timed H100 benchmarking task.

{Important Notes}:
- Tokenizer implementation surprised many students in past iterations — it can be more work than expected.
- You may prototype locally without large GPUs; only final benchmarks require cluster time.
- Small architecture changes and hyperparameter tuning can cause large performance differences, so detail matters.

discussions.title: Systems unit: kernels, parallelism, and inference
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This unit covers how to get the most from hardware: writing efficient GPU kernels, using parallelism across GPUs and nodes, and optimizing inference (running models). Students will write kernels (using Triton), learn parallelism strategies, and study inference phases and speedup tricks.

{Definitions}:
- GPU: A processor with many cores designed for parallel floating-point computations used in ML training and inference.
- Kernel: A small, performance-critical piece of code (often on GPU) that implements core operations like matrix multiply.
- Triton: A toolkit for writing custom GPU kernels more easily.
- Data parallelism: A parallelism method that splits input data across multiple workers which each hold a full copy of the model.
- Model (tensor) parallelism: A method that splits model parameters across multiple GPUs because the model is too large to fit on one device.
- Inference: Running a trained model to generate outputs (e.g., next tokens) from input prompts.
- Prefill: The inference stage where you process all tokens of the given prompt in parallel (same as training forward pass).
- Decode (autoregressive decoding): The phase that generates tokens one-by-one, where each new token depends on prior generated tokens.
- Speculative decoding: A technique that uses a cheaper model to generate several tokens ahead and then validates them with the main model to speed decoding.

{Key Concepts}:
- GPUs have fast compute units on-chip and off-chip memory; moving data is often the main bottleneck.
- Kernels must minimize data movement (tiling, fusion) to fully use GPU compute.
- Multi-GPU training needs careful partitioning of parameters, activations, and gradients to reduce communication overhead (NVLink, switches, etc.).
- Inference has two parts: prefill (parallel-friendly) and decode (sequential and harder to parallelize); decode is often memory-bound and latency-sensitive.

{Important Notes}:
- You will implement a GPU kernel and simple parallelism in assignment two; full-scale model parallelism (e.g., FSDP) is complicated to build from scratch.
- Always profile and benchmark to find real bottlenecks before optimizing.
- Inference cost globally can exceed training cost because inference repeats for every use of the model.

discussions.title: Scaling laws unit and the Chinchilla rule
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This unit teaches how to run small-scale experiments and fit scaling laws to predict model performance at larger compute budgets. The key question is how to trade off model size and data (tokens) given a fixed FLOPs budget; the Chinchilla rule gives a practical ratio.

{Definitions}:
- FLOPs budget: The total amount of floating-point operations available for training; used as a compute budget measure.
- Scaling laws: Empirical relationships that show how model loss or accuracy changes with model size, data size, and compute.
- Chinchilla optimal (rule of thumb): A result that gives the best ratio of data tokens to model parameters for a given compute budget (roughly 20 tokens per parameter in the studied regime).

{Key Concepts}:
- For a fixed compute budget, you must choose model size (parameters) and dataset size (tokens) — scaling laws show the optimal trade-off.
- Empirical studies show near-linear trends that allow extrapolation and practical rules of thumb like the Chinchilla rule.
- Assignment three provides a simulated training API: students run many small experiments, fit a scaling curve, and predict optimal hyperparameters for larger scales.

{Important Notes}:
- Scaling law predictions ignore inference cost; they optimize training loss for a given compute budget only.
- The assignment enforces a simulated FLOPs budget to teach careful experiment selection and prioritization.

discussions.title: Data unit: evaluation, curation, filtering, and deduplication
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This part focuses on data, which largely determines what a model can do. Topics include how to evaluate language models, how to collect and clean web data (Common Crawl), filtering, deduplication, and legal/ethical issues around data. Assignment four has students process raw crawl data and improve quality.

{Definitions}:
- Common Crawl: A large public web crawl dataset containing raw HTML from many websites.
- Deduplication: The process of finding and removing duplicate or near-duplicate text segments from a dataset.
- Filtering (in data curation): Removing low-quality or harmful documents from raw crawled data using classifiers or heuristics.
- Evaluation metrics (e.g., perplexity, MMLU): Standard tests and scores to measure model quality on language prediction or tasks.

{Key Concepts}:
- The web has a lot of low-quality or spam content; raw crawls must be cleaned and transformed (HTML -> text) before use.
- Filtering and deduplication improve data quality and avoid wasting compute on garbage or leaked test data.
- Evaluation should include both next-token metrics (perplexity) and task-based benchmarks (MMLU), and may consider test-time techniques like chain-of-thought.

{Important Notes}:
- Data must be actively acquired and processed; it does not simply "come from the internet" in a ready-to-use form.
- Legal constraints and licensing can limit what data is safe or allowed to use; practitioners often buy curated data for frontier models.
- Assignment four gives raw Common Crawl and asks students to build filters and dedup pipelines and then optimize perplexity under a token budget.

discussions.title: Alignment: supervised fine-tuning and learning from feedback
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This unit explains how to turn a base next-token model into a useful assistant through alignment. There are two main phases: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on prompt-response pairs, and learning from feedback (preference data, verifiers) using simpler or full reinforcement learning methods to improve behavior, safety, and instruction following.

{Definitions}:
- Base model: A model trained to predict next tokens (raw language model) before any instruction tuning or alignment.
- Alignment: The process of adjusting a base model so it follows instructions, adopts desired style, and refuses harmful outputs.
- Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): Training the model on example prompt-response pairs so it learns to produce desirable outputs.
- Preference data: Data where humans (or other systems) indicate which of two model outputs they prefer for a given prompt.
- Verifier: A mechanism (could be learned) that judges whether a model response is correct or good for a given domain (useful for math or code evaluation).
- PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization): A reinforcement learning algorithm commonly used to optimize models with reward signals.
- DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): A simpler algorithm for learning from pairwise preference data without a full RL setup.
- GRPO (Grouped Relative Preference Optimization): A variant that simplifies RL by removing the value function and was developed for efficiency in alignment.

{Key Concepts}:
- SFT is often enough to give instruction-following from a good base model; small curated datasets can be powerful.
- Preference learning uses A/B comparisons to teach models what users like without full reward labeling.
- For domains with concrete verifiers (math, code), a verifier can provide strong supervision; otherwise RL methods like PPO, DPO, or GRPO are used.
- Assignment five asks students to implement SFT and RL-from-feedback algorithms (DPO and GRPO) and evaluate results.

{Important Notes}:
- Gathering high-quality SFT data is expensive; preference data and verifiers are ways to use cheaper supervision.
- Choosing the right learning method depends on the type of feedback available (pairwise preferences vs scalar rewards vs verifiers).

discussions.title: Recap: efficiency as the driving principle and resource regimes
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
The instructor recaps that efficiency — getting the best model for a given compute and data budget — is the central idea throughout the course. Design decisions (data processing, tokenization, architectures, training regimes) depend on whether you are compute-poor or data-poor, and the course focuses on the compute-constrained regime.

{Definitions}:
- Efficiency (in this course): Maximizing model quality (accuracy, loss) per unit of compute and data.
- Compute-constrained regime: A situation where compute is the scarce resource and data is relatively abundant.

{Key Concepts}:
- When compute is limited, filter data aggressively, use tokenization for compute efficiency, and prefer training recipes that expose more unique data quickly (e.g., single epoch passes).
- If data becomes scarce (data-constrained regime), different choices might become optimal, such as doing more epochs or changing architectures.

{Important Notes}:
- The course assumes students are mostly GPU-poor (compute-limited) and teaches decisions that squeeze the most from limited hardware.
- Design choices that make sense in one regime may be suboptimal in another; always consider the resource regime when designing experiments.

discussions.title: Tokenization: role, basic definitions, and motivation
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section introduces tokenization and basic vocabulary concepts. Tokenization converts raw Unicode strings into sequences of integer token IDs that models use; choosing a tokenizer affects sequence length and model efficiency.

{Definitions}:
- Tokenizer: Converts between text strings and sequences of integers (tokens) that a model can process.
- Token: An integer code that represents a piece of text (a character, byte, or a subword piece).
- Vocabulary size: The number of distinct tokens the tokenizer can output.

{Key Concepts}:
- Tokenizers must be reversible: encoding and decoding should recover text reliably.
- Each token represents some amount of bytes; the average bytes-per-token (compression ratio) affects sequence length and compute.

{Important Notes}:
- Tokenization design has important efficiency trade-offs: shorter token sequences help reduce attention compute costs.
- Tokenizers in practice often include a convention where spaces are part of tokens (e.g., space + word is different from word without space).

discussions.title: Examples of tokenizers and compression ratio
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
The instructor shows how existing tokenizers (e.g., GPT-2 tokenizer) split strings and shows the bytes-per-token compression ratio (about 1.6 for GPT tokenization). Students are encouraged to try tokenizers and inspect how common strings are split.

{Definitions}:
- Compression ratio (bytes per token): The average number of bytes represented by one token; higher means more compression (shorter token sequences).

{Key Concepts}:
- Tokenizers often treat whitespace as part of tokens and split common words into single tokens when beneficial.
- Numeric strings and other unusual text may be split into multiple tokens; tokenization reflects corpus statistics.

{Important Notes}:
- Playing with tokenizers interactively helps build intuition about how text maps to token IDs.
- The bytes-per-token number is a useful statistic when thinking about model sequence lengths and compute.

discussions.title: Character-, byte-, and word-based tokenization: pros and cons
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This part compares simple tokenization choices: character-based, byte-based, and word-based. Each has trade-offs: character-based wastes vocabulary slots, byte-based gives consistent small vocab but long sequences, and word-based adapts well but causes an unbounded vocabulary and out-of-vocabulary issues.

{Definitions}:
- Character-based tokenization: Each Unicode character maps to a token (code point).
- Byte-based tokenization: Text is encoded as raw bytes (e.g., UTF-8 bytes) and each byte is a token (vocab size up to 256).
- Word-based tokenization: Splits text into words or segments by spaces/regex and treats each segment as a token.

{Key Concepts}:
- Character-based: small average token length but very sparse vocab and inefficient use of vocabulary slots.
- Byte-based: fixed small vocabulary (0–255) and robust to any input, but sequences become long, increasing attention cost.
- Word-based: adaptive (short tokens for common words) but has unlimited vocabulary and rare/new words cause trouble.

{Important Notes}:
- Long token sequences (from byte-based) are costly because attention computation grows with sequence length.
- Word-based systems face practical issues like unknown words and must handle out-of-vocabulary tokens carefully.

discussions.title: Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenizer: algorithm and demo
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
This section explains the BPE tokenizer algorithm: start with bytes, count common adjacent pairs, merge the most frequent pair into a new token, and repeat. The instructor walks through a small example ("cat" and "hat") to show how repeated merges compress common subsequences into tokens.

{Definitions}:
- Byte Pair Encoding (BPE): A simple iterative algorithm that builds a tokenizer by repeatedly merging the most frequent adjacent pair of symbols (initially bytes) into a new token.
- Merge table: A record of which symbol pairs have been merged and what new token they map to.

{Key Concepts}:
- BPE learns token pieces from data, allocating vocabulary capacity to frequent byte sequences so common chunks become single tokens.
- The algorithm reduces sequence length as new tokens represent longer sequences of bytes, improving compression and efficiency.
- Practical BPE implementations pre-tokenize text (e.g., by word-like splits) for efficiency and add special tokens and faster encode implementations.

{Important Notes}:
- A naive BPE implementation can be slow if it loops inefficiently over all merges; practical implementations optimize encode/decode.
- Real tokenizers add pre-tokenization steps and special tokens; students will implement a performant BPE variant in the assignment.

discussions.title: Tokenization summary and next steps
discussions.summary:
{Summarize content}:
The instructor summarizes tokenization: it maps text to integer sequences, BPE is the practical and widely used approach today, and tokenization remains necessary until byte-level models become efficient at frontier scale. Next lecture will focus on PyTorch primitives and resource accounting.

{Definitions}:
- Tokenization (recap): The process of turning Unicode text into tokens (integers) that models can use.

{Key Concepts}:
- BPE is an effective, data-driven, and historic algorithm still commonly used for tokenization.
- Tokenization choices strongly affect compute efficiency, so they are an important engineering decision.

{Important Notes}:
- Even if byte-level models are elegant, current architectures make tokenization an important efficiency lever.
- Next lecture: detailed PyTorch building blocks and careful counting of FLOPs and memory use.
